title: Logassa (Caturviṁśati-stava) — Praise of the Twenty-Four Tīrthaṅkaras
summary: Logassa is a short hymn of praise to the twenty-four Tīrthaṅkaras — the great teachers who, in each cosmic age, rediscover and re-establish the path to liberation. In seven verses the reciter first vows to praise them, then names all twenty-four in order, and finally asks these perfected ones for health, the gift of enlightenment (bodhi), and a calm, undisturbed mind. It is one of the six daily obligations (āvaśyaka) of a Jain practitioner, and is recited in every pratikramaṇa. Because it has a fixed, well-known length, it also serves as the standard 'measuring unit' for timed standing meditation (kāyotsarga).
ritualPlacement: Logassa is the anchor sūtra of the Second Āvaśyaka — Cauvīsattho (Sanskrit Caturviṁśati-stava, 'praise of the twenty-four'). In the Devasi (daily) pratikramaṇa it is recited after the Iriyāvahiyaṁ confession and its one-Logassa kāyotsarga, and it recurs, unchanged in wording, across all five pratikramaṇas (devasi, rāi, pakṣik, cāturmāsika, sāṁvatsarī). Note two distinct uses that newcomers often conflate: (a) Logassa recited aloud as the Second Āvaśyaka, and (b) Logassa held silently as the unit that measures kāyotsarga — one Logassa is meditated 'up to chandesu nimmalayara' (the start of verse 7), i.e. the first six-and-a-bit verses, counted at twenty-five breaths. The graded kāyotsarga measures of the longer pratikramaṇas are stated in Logassa-units: 12 Logassa (Pakṣik), 20 (Cāturmāsika), 40 Logassa + 1 Navkar (Sāṁvatsarī). The well-known pakkhī → cāumāsī → saṁvatsarī word-substitution rule belongs to the surrounding vidhi, not to the wording of Logassa itself, which is identical wherever it appears.
authorAndSource: Logassa is a canonical liturgical text embedded in the Āvaśyaka-sūtra (the second of the six Āvaśyakas), not an authored devotional composition; like the other core Āvaśyaka formulae it is traditionally regarded as ancient and of anonymous/collective origin, transmitted within the Ardhamāgadhī Prakrit canon. The Āvaśyaka literature is associated in tradition with Bhadrabāhu (Niryukti) and was classically expounded by Haribhadrasūri and later commentators. The language is Ardhamāgadhī Prakrit. The Anchalgachchha (Vidhipakṣa) recension followed here prints the verses verbatim from the source book Pañca Pratikramaṇa Sūtra – Vidhi Sahit, whose orthography (anusvāras, conjuncts, mātrās) is authoritative — including its internal spelling variants such as 'caüvvīsaṁpi' (v.1) versus 'caüvīsaṁpi' (v.5).
structureArc: The seven verses move from intention, to naming, to petition. v.1 is the resolve to praise: the Tīrthaṅkaras are hailed as illuminators of the world (logassa ujjoagare), makers of the dharma-ford (dhamma-titthayare), Jinas, Arihantas and Kevalīs — 'I will praise (kittaïssaṁ) all twenty-four.' vv.2–4 are the roll-call of all twenty-four in canonical order: v.2 names 1–8 (Ṛṣabha through Candraprabha), v.3 names 9–16 (Suvidhi/Puṣpadanta through Śānti), v.4 names 17–24 (Kunthu through Vardhamāna/Mahāvīra). v.5 closes the praise and turns it into petition: 'thus praised by me, freed of the rust of karma (vihuya-raya-malā), beyond aging and death — may the twenty-four Jinavaras be gracious to me (pasīyantu).' v.6 broadens the request: those supreme Siddhas, now praised, venerated and worshipped (kittiya vandiya mahiyā), are asked to grant health (ārogya), the gain of enlightenment (bodhi-lābha) and the highest excellent samādhi. v.7 seals the hymn with three similes of the Siddhas' surpassing purity and depth — purer than the moons, more radiant than the suns, profound as the great ocean — 'may the Siddhas grant me perfection (siddhi).'
recurringImagery: Three image-fields run through the hymn and are explained here once so the per-verse notes need not re-gloss them. (1) Light / illumination: the Tīrthaṅkaras are 'illuminators of the world' (logassa ujjoagare, v.1), and the closing verse intensifies this — they are 'purer than the moons' (candesu nimmalayarā) and 'more illuminating than the suns' (āiccesu ahiyaṁ payāsayarā, v.7); spiritual knowledge (kevala-jñāna) is figured as a light surpassing the brightest natural lights. (2) The ford / crossing (tīrtha): a Tīrthaṅkara is literally a 'ford-maker' (dhamma-titthayare, v.1) — one who builds the crossing-place over the ocean of worldly existence (saṁsāra); verse 7's simile of the Siddhas as 'deep as the great ocean' (sāgara-vara-gambhīrā) answers this, the practitioner seeking the far shore (siddhi). (3) Cleansing of karmic 'rust': liberation is imaged as metal scoured clean — 'freed of the rust-stain (of karma)' (vihuya-raya-malā, v.5) and so 'beyond aging and death' (pahīṇa-jara-maraṇā). Together these say: the Jinas are light to see by, a ford to cross by, and proof that the soul can be cleansed and made deathless.
keyThemes: Naming the Jinas in canonical order, Ṛṣabha to Mahāvīra
The Tīrthaṅkara as illuminator and ford-maker
Liberation as removal of karmic 'rust' and freedom from aging and death
Petition for health, bodhi (enlightenment) and supreme samādhi
The Siddhas' purity beyond moon and sun; depth like the ocean
Logassa as the Second Āvaśyaka and as the unit of kāyotsarga

glossary.definition: 'Praise of the twenty-four'; the formal name of this hymn and of the Second Āvaśyaka it anchors.
Prakrit name of the Second Āvaśyaka — the obligatory recitation honouring the twenty-four Tīrthaṅkaras.
A 'ford-maker': one of the twenty-four supreme teachers who re-found the fourfold community and the path across the ocean of rebirth.
'Victor' — one who has conquered the inner passions; a synonym for the Tīrthaṅkaras (intensified as jinavara, 'best of Jinas').
'Worthy one' / destroyer of inner foes; the living, fully-awakened Tīrthaṅkara, first of the five Parameṣṭhins.
One possessing kevala-jñāna, omniscient infinite knowledge; every Tīrthaṅkara is a kevalin.
A liberated, perfected soul freed from all karma and rebirth; the goal invoked in vv.6–7.
'Abandonment of the body': motionless standing meditation; its duration is measured in silent Logassa-recitations.
An 'obligatory' daily observance; there are six, of which Logassa is the second.
'Attainment of enlightenment'; the gift of right insight/awakening petitioned in v.6 (Prakrit bohi-lābha).
Composed, undisturbed equanimity of mind; 'the supreme excellent samādhi' sought in v.6.
Health — here both bodily wellbeing and, by extension, the soul's 'health' of liberation, asked for in v.6.
Praise, extolling; the verb kittaïssaṁ (v.1) is the reciter's vow 'I will praise'.
'Having shaken off the rust-stain (of karma)'; the epithet in v.5 imaging liberation as cleansed metal.
Gracious favour; the optative pasīyantu (v.5), 'may they be gracious to me'.

howToRead: A newcomer can read Logassa simply as a heartfelt litany — vow to praise, name the twenty-four teachers in order, then ask them for health, awakening and peace of mind — and follow the moon/sun/ocean images of verse 7 as pictures of their greatness. An advanced practitioner should attend to the precise Ardhamāgadhī epithets (ujjoagare, dhamma-titthayare, vihuya-raya-malā), to Logassa's double role as Second Āvaśyaka and as the breath-counted unit of kāyotsarga, and to the verbatim orthography of this Anchalgachchha recension, including its deliberate spelling variants.

sources.title: Pañca Pratikramaṇa Sūtra – Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha / Vidhipakṣa edition)
sources.consultedFor: Authoritative verbatim verse text, orthography, ritual placement and kāyotsarga Logassa-counts.

sources.title: Paul Dundas, The Jains (2nd ed., Routledge)
sources.consultedFor: Context of the Āvaśyaka rite, the Cauvīsattho, and the twenty-four Tīrthaṅkaras.

sources.title: Avashyaka Sutra (Āvaśyaka-sūtra), Jain Āgama literature
sources.consultedFor: Identification of Logassa as the Second Āvaśyaka (Caturviṁśati-stava) within the Āvaśyaka canon.

notes: Logassa is canonical/anonymous; the attribution of the Āvaśyaka Niryukti to Bhadrabāhu and the expository tradition of Haribhadrasūri concern the surrounding Āvaśyaka literature, not authorship of these seven gāthās. Tīrthaṅkara order is standard; sectarian note: the Anchalgachchha is Śvetāmbara Mūrtipūjaka, which counts the 19th Tīrthaṅkara Malli as female (named in v.4). The kāyotsarga 'one Logassa = twenty-five breaths, recited up to chandesu nimmalayara' and the 12/20/40-Logassa measures follow the book and Āvaśyaka Niryukti g.1532 as cited therein. The source book's verbatim spelling variants (e.g. caüvvīsaṁpi v.1 vs caüvīsaṁpi v.5) are preserved deliberately and must not be normalised.
